policy_statement: By 2023, evaluate rate and program options to facilitate an affordable transition to all-electric buildings (ES.3-2).
mechanism_description: The city will evaluate rate designs and program options to support an affordable transition to all-electric buildings, reducing emissions from on-site fossil fuel use.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy service provision (heat/cooking) → fuel switch (gas/oil → electricity) → ↓ on-site fossil combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human/system activity: Building owners/developers retrofit or construct buildings with electric end-use equipment (e.g., heat pumps, electric resistance where applicable, induction stoves) instead of gas/oil/propane systems; sometimes coupled with panel upgrades and wiring readiness.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of methane/oil in boilers/furnaces/water heaters and associated CO₂ (and some CH₄ leakage upstream) emissions; emissions may shift to centralized power generation, where they are typically lower per unit service and can decline over time with grid decarbonization.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in building-related GHG emissions (especially Scope 1), contributing to overall emissions mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is eliminating on-site fossil fuel combustion in buildings, thereby reducing direct GHG emissions (and often enabling further reductions as electricity supply decarbonizes).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Switching building end uses from fossil fuels to electricity reduces direct combustion emissions, which is a direct emissions mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While electrification can also improve energy efficiency (heat pumps) and local air quality, the mechanism as stated is fundamentally about reducing GHGs by eliminating on-site fossil fuel use; that is Mitigation rather than Resource Efficiency (which would be framed as “same service with less energy input” irrespective of fuel).  
3) Edge cases applied: If the electricity supply is very carbon-intensive, near-term net GHG benefits can be smaller or delayed; nevertheless, the intent and primary mechanism remain emissions reduction via fuel switching, often paired with grid decarbonization assumptions.  
4) Primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy under “building electrification → building emissions reduction” shares the same core mitigation pathway—reducing fossil fuel combustion in buildings—regardless of whether it targets new builds, retrofits, incentives, or rate/program design.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a specific policy is code-based, incentive-based, public-sector procurement, or equity-focused (and whether it also materially increases efficiency or resilience) requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG impact can depend on grid emissions intensity and peak generation mix; some electrification measures may be pursued primarily for health/indoor air quality or cost reasons, but the canonical mechanism’s dominant climate effect remains mitigation via reduced on-site fossil combustion. In rare contexts with highly carbon-intensive grids and no decarbonization trajectory, short-run emissions could increase, but this does not change the mechanism’s primary classification.
key_indicators: “By 2023, evaluate”; “rate and program options”; “facilitate an affordable transition to all-electric buildings”
co_benefits: Potential energy affordability/consumer bill impacts; indoor air quality and public health from reduced combustion; potential grid management benefits depending on rate design (e.g., load shifting)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an incentive-program (if “program options” implies rebates/assistance) or pricing-mechanism (if “rate options” implies tariff reform), but the operative verb is “evaluate,” so it is best classified as a planning/strategy instrument rather than implementation.
